aliases: Croissant breakfast sandwich with bacon and egg

english: Croissant breakfast sandwich with bacon and egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn class for a bacon-egg-cheese croissant exists; mapped to the nearest parent class 'sandwich food product' (FOODON_00002473) as a defensible approximate anchor. A croissant-based breakfast sandwich is a filled pastry product analogous to a sandwich.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers plain croissant. No composite filled-croissant sandwich code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue; the croissant base code is used as the closest defensible anchor for this composite breakfast item.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for a bacon-egg-cheese croissant composite product. SNOMED CT food coverage for composite fast-food items is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one assembled croissant sandwich weighing approximately 150 g, comprising a full-size butter croissant, one fried egg, two to three rashers of bacon, and one slice of processed cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Bacon, egg and cheese croissant — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Bacon, egg and cheese croissant' (cr_id F08020001). All per-100g macros, micronutrients, and minerals are taken verbatim from this record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for a bacon-egg-cheese croissant (USDA SR Legacy #21318 and component data) as HPB does not report saturated fat separately; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; croissant GI reference (GI ~67, white-flour croissant)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite bacon-egg-cheese croissant. GI of 67 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain butter croissant (white flour, GI ~67, medium category). The protein and fat from bacon, egg, and cheese will attenuate postprandial glucose response in practice, so the true composite GI is likely lower; however, the croissant-base GI is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (67 × 24.34 g/100g × 150 g) / 100 = (67 × 36.51 g) / 100 = 24.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (474 mg/100 g) from cured bacon and processed cheese, with negligible potassium (13 mg/100 g) providing no offsetting effect
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this item; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 711 mg sodium (nearly 50% of the 1500 mg daily target for hypertensives), and the very low potassium-to-sodium ratio further elevates cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined high-GI croissant base (GI ~67) with low dietary fibre (1.74 g/100 g) promotes rapid postprandial glucose rise; trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) worsens insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this item or substitute the croissant with a high-fibre wholegrain wrap and limit portion size, as the refined pastry base and low fibre content will cause a rapid and sustained blood glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~8.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) from butter croissant and processed cheese elevate LDL cholesterol; high sodium (474 mg/100 g) contributes to arterial hypertension
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this item due to the combination of high saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium, all of which independently and synergistically increase the risk of atherosclerosis, myocardial infarction, and stroke.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is a hard pro-inflammatory trigger; saturated fat (~8.5 g/100 g) and processed red meat (bacon) further amplify inflammatory pathways via arachidonic acid
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this item entirely, as the combination of trans fat, high saturated fat, and processed cured pork is strongly pro-inflammatory and may exacerbate joint inflammation and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A bacon, egg and cheese croissant is a Western-style breakfast sandwich consisting of a flaky, buttery laminated croissant pastry filled with a fried egg, one or more rashers of cured bacon, and a slice of processed or melted cheese. The croissant provides a rich, layered, slightly crisp exterior with a soft, airy interior, while the bacon contributes a salty, smoky, savoury flavour and the egg adds creaminess and protein. The cheese melts into the warm filling, binding the components and adding a mild dairy richness. This item is commonly consumed as a breakfast or working-lunch meal in Singapore cafes, bakeries, and fast-food outlets. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 307 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate-to-high protein content (14.45 g/100 g) and high fat content (18.36 g/100 g), of which approximately 8.5 g is saturated fat and 0.2 g is trans fat from the croissant pastry. Sodium is high at 474 mg/100 g, driven by cured bacon and processed cheese. Dietary fibre is low at 1.74 g/100 g. Clinically, this item is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension (high sodium, low potassium), Type 2 Diabetes (high-GI refined pastry, low fibre, trans fat worsening insulin resistance), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (trans fat and processed red meat are pro-inflammatory). It is best consumed occasionally and in small portions, ideally paired with a high-fibre vegetable side.
common_user_queries: Is a bacon egg and cheese croissant healthy?
How many calories in a bacon egg cheese croissant?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08020001, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for a comparable bacon-egg-cheese croissant and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain butter croissant.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (8.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (6.2 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g), and trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g) are not reported separately in the HPB Foodo source; these values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for a comparable bacon-egg-cheese croissant composite and are consistent with the total fat of 18.36 g/100 g (sum: 8.5 + 6.2 + 1.8 + 0.2 = 16.7 g; the remaining ~1.66 g represents minor fatty acid fractions not individually reported). 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this item, and detailed vitamin and mineral profiles for this composite food are not available in the HPB or USDA FoodData Central records consulted as of 2026-04-16. 3. Glycemic load per serving (150 g): GL = (67 × (24.34 × 1.50)) / 100 = (67 × 36.51) / 100 = 24.5. 4. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a Western-origin composite breakfast item. No mandatory non-English local-language names are required under the multilingual rules for this cuisine category. 5. Cholesterol value of 4.4 mg/100 g from HPB source appears unusually low for a bacon-egg-cheese croissant (typical values 150–200 mg/100 g); this value is preserved as reported by the approved source and flagged for review. 6. Potassium value of 13 mg/100 g from HPB source appears unusually low; preserved as reported.
